10.1016/j.jmp.2024.102897
In recent years, with the continuous development of educational modernization, personalized teaching has become an important direction in current educational development. The traditional one-size-fits-all education model is no longer able to meet the personalized needs of students. Therefore, how to provide personalized learning path recommendations (how to arrange hierarchical and personalized assignments, completing consolidation learning recommendations) has become an urgent issues to be addressed. In current related research on personalized learning path recommendations, the purpose is to customize a learning path for individual learners that adhere to educational principles, is practical, feasible, and scientifically efficient. This approach assists learners in attaining their learning objectives to the fullest extent while minimizing associated learning costs.

Personalized learning path recommendation is a research topic that involves interdisciplinary studies in education, psychology, computer science, and other fields. Its research approaches and methods can be roughly categorized into three types: education theory-driven learning analytics (LA) methods, data-driven educational data mining (EDM) methods, and hybrid methods that combine the first two approaches ( Aldowah, Al-Samarraie, & Fauzy, 2019; Siemens & Baker, 2012 ). In this context, learning analytics methods driven by educational theory primarily concentrates on the examination of educational principles, offering the benefit of robust interpretability. However, they also come with some drawbacks, including limited portability, elevated costs, and a substantial impact from researchers’ subjective factors. On the other hand, data-driven educational data mining methods aim to unveil inherent patterns in the personalized learning path recommendations from extensive data, providing advantages such as strong portability and cost-effectiveness. Nevertheless, some accompanied shortcomings should be considered, including low interpretability and challenges in convincing experts in fields such as education. Therefore, researchers combine the two to form a hybrid methods. It is undeniable that compared to data-driven educational data mining methods, a mature, systematic, scientific, and highly interpretable educational theory is more difficult to obtain, and the theoretical foundation of this paper — the knowledge space theory — is such a scientific and effective theoretical framework.

Furthermore, many scholars have already conducted relevant research:  Doignon and Falmagne (1985)  and  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  proposed definitions for the outer fringe, inner fringe, and learning paths in knowledge space theory. Teachers can utilize the outer fringe for learning guidance or learning path recommendations and utilize the inner fringe for reinforcement learning recommendations (including assigning homework). Addressing the shortcomings of the theory, many researchers ( Doignon, 1994; Düntsch & Gediga, 1995; Falmagne, Koppen, Villano, Doignon, & Johannesen, 1990; Gediga & Düntsch, 2002; Heller, Ünlü, & Albert, 2013; Korossy, 1997, 1999 ) collectively proposed the competence-based knowledge space theory. Subsequently,  Anselmi, Heller, Stefanutti, and Robusto (2022, 2024), Heller, Anselmi, Stefanutti, and Robusto (2017), Heller, Stefanutti, Anselmi, and Robusto (2015, 2016)  and  Heller, Steiner, Hockemeyer, and Albert (2006)  proposed methods for obtaining competence state in competence-based knowledge space theory and using it to find learning paths. However, this method requires introducing additional information or changing the problem set to obtain a competence state.  Stefanutti and de Chiusole (2017)  proposed a method in competence-based knowledge space theory specifically targeting the conjunctive model for finding learning paths under the condition of not knowing the specific competence state. However, this method only considers the special case where the effective outer fringe is non-empty. Meanwhile, in subsequent research on the knowledge space theory, we observe that the majority of researchers tend to focus solely on the outer fringe, neglecting the inner fringe. This is because most properties of the inner and outer fringes have simple correspondences, but the application of the inner fringe should not be overlooked.

Nabizadeh, Leal, Rafsanjani, and Shah (2020)  categorize personalized learning path recommendation methods into two main classes: Course Generation (CG) and Course Sequence (CS). The methods of CG generate and recommend the entire path to a user in a single recommendation, with learning assessment occurring only after completing the path. The methods of CS generate and recommend a path to a user step by step, taking into account the user’s progress, and the learning assessment happens as the user progresses along the path. The recommendation method based on knowledge space theory belongs to CS, requiring personalized learning guidance step by step until the learner achieves the learning goals. Hence, the focus of this paper’s research will be on how to provide learning guidance.

In knowledge space theory, teachers assess students’ current knowledge states, outer fringes, and inner fringes based on their responses to test questions. Teachers can propose the next problems for students to solve based on the outer fringe and guide their next steps of learning, thereby planning students’ learning paths. Teachers can also identify the problems that students need to consolidate based on the inner fringe and assign corresponding homework. However, the outer fringe of the knowledge state may be empty, meaning that students in this knowledge state cannot make progress by studying just one question. Similarly, the inner fringe of the knowledge state may also be empty, indicating that students in this knowledge state cannot consolidate their knowledge state by reviewing just one question. Therefore, many scholars confine their research to well-graded knowledge spaces, which are a type of knowledge structure where each knowledge state has a non-empty outer (inner) fringe. Additionally,  Noventa, Spoto, Heller, and Kelava (2019)  expanded the concept of the outer fringe, no longer limiting it to learning only one question at a time, which better reflects real-world situations. We refer to this as the outer master fringe, and correspondingly, we can define the inner master fringe.

Based on the above discussion, we can identify a limitation of knowledge space theory, which is that students should learn the skills and knowledge necessary to solve problems, rather than the problems themselves. Therefore, Falmagne et al. proposed competence-based knowledge space theory, elaborating on it extensively in  Heller et al. (2013) , which introduces skills into knowledge space theory. Similarly, this theory proposes the next skill for students to learn based on the outer fringe of competence state and identifies the skill for students to consolidate based on the inner fringe of competence state. However, since skills cannot be directly observed, the only way to determine whether a skill has been learned (consolidated) is by observing changes in knowledge state. However, in practical situations, students with varying competences might display identical knowledge states. Consequently, even if a skill is acquired or fails to be solidified (forgotten), the knowledge state may remain unchanged. Therefore, in the competence-based knowledge space theory, our emphasis lies on the effective (outer, inner) fringe of the competence state — comprising all skills capable of inducing alterations in the knowledge state. The change in knowledge state is inseparable from its fringes, and often, mastering a skill may solve multiple problems simultaneously. Therefore, whether introducing the concept of skills or not, acquiring the outer (inner) master fringe of the knowledge state is a crucial task.

However,  Noventa et al. (2019)  only define the master fringe, with its characterization limited to the performance level. That is, for a certain knowledge state  
                         K 
                       within the knowledge structure, its master fringe is determined by comparing it with adjacent states. Furthermore, there is no further research, let alone integration with competences. And this article provides a new characterization of the master fringe based on the perspective of competence. The new characterization here is mainly reflected in the fact that for a given knowledge state, corresponding skills can be pushed for different models to reach its adjacent state or consolidate the current state. Especially via the competence model, we identify a more reasonable conception of the master fringe. Specifically, for a knowledge state  
                         K 
                      , when considering competences, its next state may experience a jump situation, which means that it can directly reach a higher-level knowledge state  
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       without passing through its adjacent knowledge state  
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                      , where  
                         K 
                       is contained in  
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       is contained in  
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                      . This enables us to provide more detailed and accurate competence-based personalized learning guidance and reinforcement learning recommendations. Therefore, this paper aims, in the competence-based knowledge space theory, via the disjunctive, conjunctive, and competence models respectively, to propose a general method to directly compute the outer (inner) master fringe of the knowledge state based on the top or bottom of the equivalence class of competence state, and a general method for personalized learning path recommendation (reinforcement learning recommendation).

The organizational structure of this paper is as follows: Section  2  provides an overview of some basic concepts in knowledge space theory and competence-based knowledge space theory. Section  3 , via the competence models, presents two characterization theorems (one characterizing the top or bottom of competence state using skill functions; another characterizing the master fringe of knowledge state using problem functions) and corresponding learning guidance (reinforcement learning recommendation) schemes. Additionally, special cases of these theorems under the disjunctive and conjunctive models are provided. Section  4  presents a real-world example. Section  5  concludes the paper.

In 1985, the mathematical psychologists Falmagne and Doignon first proposed the knowledge space theory, which provides a scientific and effective theoretical framework for educational assessment and learning guidance. This section will introduce the basic concepts of knowledge space theory, combining literature ( Falmagne & Doignon, 2011; Heller et al., 2013; Stefanutti, 2019; Stefanutti & de Chiusole, 2017 ).

The core concepts of knowledge space theory are knowledge state and knowledge structure: a knowledge structure is a pair  
                         
                            (

                            ,

                         Q 
                       and the empty set

                      . The set  
                         Q 
                       is called the problem domain of the knowledge structure, and its elements are referred to as problems or items. The subsets of  
                         Q 
                       in the family  
                         K 
                       are called knowledge states. When we refer to  
                         K 
                       as a knowledge structure on the set  
                         Q 
                      , we are referring to

As knowledge space theory evolves, many researchers realize that students should learn the skills and knowledge necessary to solve problems, rather than the problems themselves. To address this, many researchers collectively proposed a competence-based knowledge space theory, which introduces skills into knowledge space theory and distinguishes two parallel and interdependent levels: a performance level and a competence level. The knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K

                         C 
                       are referred to as competence states. These two levels are connected by two maps, referred to as the skill function (skill map, skill multimap) and the problem function. For simplification, unless otherwise specified, all the competence structures

The representation of disjunctive and conjunctive skill functions can be simplified by introducing the symbol of the skill map ( Heller et al., 2013; Stefanutti & de Chiusole, 2017 ). A skill map is a triple  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            τ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , where  
                         Q 
                       is a non-empty finite problem domain,  
                         S 
                       is a non-empty finite set of skills corresponding to  
                         Q 
                      , and  
                         τ 
                       is a mapping from  
                         Q 
                       to  
                         
                            
                               
                                  2 
                               
                               
                                  S 
                               
                            
                            ∖ 
                            
                               { 
                               0̸ 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      . For any  
                         q 
                       in  
                         Q 
                      , the subset  
                         
                            τ 
                            
                               ( 
                               q 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       of  
                         S 
                       will be called the set of skills assigned to  
                         q 
                       (according to the skill map  
                         τ 
                      ). When the specific content of the sets  
                         Q 
                       and  
                         S 
                       is defined, sometimes we directly refer to  
                         τ 
                       itself as a skill map. Using the concept of skill map, the disjunctive skill function  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            μ 
                            ) 
                         
                       mentioned earlier can be equivalently represented by the skill map  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            τ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , where for  
                         
                            ∀ 
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                      , it satisfies  
                         
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               q 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               
                                  { 
                                  s 
                                  } 
                               
                               ∣ 
                               s 
                               ∈ 
                               τ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  q 
                                  ) 
                               
                               } 
                            
                         
                      ; similarly, the conjunctive skill function  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            μ 
                            ) 
                         
                       can be equivalently represented by the skill map  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            τ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , where for  
                         
                            ∀ 
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                      , it satisfies  
                         
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               q 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               τ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  q 
                                  ) 
                               
                               } 
                            
                         
                      . This leads to the equivalent representation of disjunctive and conjunctive problem functions. For any skill subset  
                         
                            T 
                            ⊆ 
                            S 
                         
                      , the disjunctive problem function induced by the disjunctive skill function  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            τ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is  
                         
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               T 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               q 
                               ∈ 
                               Q 
                               ∣ 
                               τ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  q 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ∩ 
                               T 
                               ≠ 
                               0̸ 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      ; and the conjunctive problem function induced by the conjunctive skill function  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            τ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is  
                         
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               T 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               q 
                               ∈ 
                               Q 
                               ∣ 
                               τ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  q 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ⊆ 
                               T 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      . For any skill subset  
                         
                            T 
                            ⊆ 
                            S 
                         
                      , let  
                         
                            K 
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               T 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       denotes the knowledge state delineated by the competence state  
                         T 
                       via the disjunctive model, and let  
                         
                            K

                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     d 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  T 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ∣ 
                               T 
                               ⊆ 
                               S 
                               } 
                            
                         
                       denotes the knowledge space delineated by the skill map  
                         τ

                            K

                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  c

                               T

                       denotes the simple closure space delineated by the skill map

Example 2

For disjunctive and conjunctive problem functions, the following conclusions are obviously established:

                                  ⊆ 
                                  S

Suppose  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        d 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a disjunctive problem function, and

                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  ⊆ 
                                  S

(1) If  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  ⊆ 
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                            , then  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        d

                                           T

                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ⊆ 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        d

                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             
                            and

                                     )

                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                            
                            .

(2)  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        d 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     ∪ 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        d

                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ∪ 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        d

                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        c

                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     ∩ 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ∩ 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                            
                            .

In the knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                       is the set family of all knowledge states in  
                         K 
                       containing the element  
                         q 
                      , that is  
                         
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               K 
                               ∈ 
                               K 
                               ∣ 
                               q 
                               ∈ 
                               K 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      .  
                         
                            
                               q 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                       is the set of all elements in  
                         Q 
                       that always appear together with the element  
                         q 
                       in the same state, that is  
                         
                            
                               
                                  q 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               r 
                               ∈ 
                               Q 
                               ∣ 
                               
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                                  
                                     r 
                                  
                               
                               } 
                            
                         
                      , and is called a notion of the knowledge structure. If all notions in the knowledge structure contain only one element, then the knowledge structure is called discriminative.

In a discriminative knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                      , let  
                         
                            K 
                            ∈ 
                            K 
                         
                       be a knowledge state. Then, the outer fringe of  
                         K 
                       is the problem set  
                         
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  O 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               q 
                               ∈ 
                               Q 
                               ∖ 
                               K 
                               ∣ 
                               K 
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  q 
                                  } 
                               
                               ∈ 
                               K 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      , and the inner fringe of  
                         K 
                       is the problem set  
                         
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  I 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               q 
                               ∈ 
                               K 
                               ∣ 
                               K 
                               ∖ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  q 
                                  } 
                               
                               ∈ 
                               K 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      . From the perspective of education, the outer fringe of  
                         K 
                       represents the zone of proximal development for students with knowledge state  
                         K 
                      , which is the set of problems the students are about to learn with their existing knowledge. The inner fringe of  
                         K 
                       represents the high point of a student’s knowledge state. This is the set of problems that the student has recently mastered, which may not be fully solidified and may require review and reinforcement. However, the outer (inner) fringe of a knowledge state may be empty. For example, consider the discriminative knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                      , where  
                         
                            Q 
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               a 
                               , 
                               b 
                               , 
                               c 
                               , 
                               d 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            K 
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               0̸ 
                               , 
                               
                                  { 
                                  c 
                                  } 
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  { 
                                  d 
                                  } 
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  { 
                                  b 
                                  , 
                                  d 
                                  } 
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  { 
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  c 
                                  , 
                                  d 
                                  } 
                               
                               , 
                               Q 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      , it is noted that  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     { 
                                     c 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  O 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            0̸ 
                         
                       ( 
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     { 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     c 
                                     , 
                                     d 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  I 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            0̸ 
                         
                      ). This means that students in this knowledge state have no way to make progress by learning only one problem (they have no way to reinforce their knowledge by reviewing only one problem). Therefore, to more effectively guide the next step of student learning,  Noventa et al. (2019)  extended  
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               O 
                            
                         
                       to  
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               OM 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               OM 
                            
                         
                      , after our slight adjustments, is defined as follows.

The following symbols frequently appear in this text. For ease of narration, they are hereby uniformly explained:

1. In the set family  
                         
                            B 
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               B 
                               ∣ 
                               B 
                               satisfies condition 
                               β 
                               , 
                               B 
                               is minimal 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      , the term “minimal” indicates that for any  
                         
                            B 
                            ∈ 
                            B 
                         
                      , there does not exist a subset  
                         
                            
                               
                                  B 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                            ⊂ 
                            B 
                         
                       that satisfies condition  
                         β 
                      . Similarly, the term “maximal” follows the same logic.

2. Let the quintuple  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            τ 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       represent a disjunctive model, where  
                         
                            K 
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     d 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  T 
                                  )

                               T 
                               ⊆ 
                               S 
                               }

                            Q

                            K

                       represent the corresponding conjunctive model and competence model, respectively.

Because the conjunctive (disjunctive) problem function satisfies closure under intersection (union), the equivalence classes of the competence states via the conjunctive (disjunctive) model have unique bottoms (tops), which can serve as representatives for further study. The definition of bottoms (tops) is as follows:

In the most general case, the problem function  
                         p 
                       of an arbitrary competence model satisfies neither closure under intersection nor closure under union. The equivalence classes of the competence states in the competence model do not have unique tops or bottoms, unlike in the disjunctive model and conjunctive model. Therefore, for the competence model, we can only settle for second best and study the bottom space and the top space, defined as follows.

                             separately, it remains crucial. More precisely, we treat

                                     ℬ 
                                  
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                               
                             for the competence state  
                               T 
                             ( Theorem 4 ) and the outer master fringe

                                     OM 
                                  
                               
                             for the knowledge state  
                               
                                  K 
                                  = 
                                  p 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     T 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             ( Theorem 7 ). If we add minimal conditions to the elements of

                            , we have

                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           ′ 
                                        
                                     
                                     is minimal 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            . This can be easily proven by  Lemma 1  and  Remark 4  (2). This is sufficient for characterizing the outer master fringe. Since the elements of the outer master fringe also require minimality, we do not impose the minimality condition on the elements of  
                               
                                  
                                     ℬ 
                                  
                                  
                                     T

Theorem 5 
                         
                            Let

                                  Q 
                                  ,

                                  p

                            be a competence model, and let  
                            
                               
                                  K 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                               
                             
                            be a knowledge state. Then, the top space of the competence state  
                            
                               T 
                             
                            satisfying  
                            
                               
                                  K 
                                  = 
                                  p 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     T 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             
                            can be characterized as:

                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     T

                            is as shown in  
                            
                               Lemma  2

Let

Proof 
                         Let

                         (1) Similarly to the proof (1) of  Theorem 4 , we can prove

                         (2) On the other hand, for any

                                           q

                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            . The theorem is proved. □

The following will be based on  Lemmas 1 and 2 ,  Theorems 4 and 5 , to utilize the problem function for characterizing the (competence-based) outer/inner master fringe of the knowledge state in the competence model. Moreover, according to  Definitions 1 and 2 , we know that the elements of these master fringes, denoted as  
                         B 
                      , must be non-empty and minimal, as our focus is on the most likely changes in the knowledge state of students. Here, “most likely” corresponds to “minimal”, and “changes” corresponds to “non-empty”. In particular, when the knowledge state is the entire problem domain  
                         Q 
                      , there are no elements in the outer master fringe. Similarly, when the knowledge state is the empty set, there are no elements in the inner master fringe.

Theorem 7 
                         
                            Let  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  , 
                                  p 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            be a competence model, and let  
                            
                               
                                  K 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                               
                             
                            be a knowledge state. Then, the outer master fringe of  
                            
                               K 
                             
                            can be characterized as:  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              K 
                                           
                                           
                                              OM 
                                           
                                        
                                        = 
                                        
                                           { 
                                        
                                        B 
                                        ∣ 
                                        B 
                                        = 
                                        p 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              
                                                 T 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ′

(2) In the result of (1), where both sides of the equation simultaneously require  
                               B 
                             to be non-empty and minimal, then by  Definition 1 , the proof is evident. □

If the  
                         
                            
                               ℬ 
                            
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               + 
                            
                         
                       in the above theorem is replaced by  
                         
                            
                               ℬ 
                            
                            
                               T 
                            
                         
                      , the conclusion may not hold, as shown in the following example.

Example 5 
                         Continued From  Example 3 
                         
                         For the knowledge state  
                               
                                  K 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     b 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            , take  
                               
                                  T 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     s 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            . Obviously,  
                               
                                  p 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     T 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  K 
                               
                            . By  Definition 1 , we know that  
                               
                                  B 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     a 
                                     } 
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        OM 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  K 
                                  ∪ 
                                  B 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     } 
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                               
                            . However, for

Moreover, we can observe that when the student’s knowledge state is  
                         
                            K 
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               b 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      , their competence state can only be

                            s 
                            } 
                         
                       or  
                         
                            { 
                            s 
                            , 
                            u 
                            } 
                         
                      . In the knowledge space theory, they can reach the new knowledge state  
                         
                            { 
                            a 
                            , 
                            b 
                            } 
                         
                       by solving problem  
                         
                            { 
                            a 
                            }

                            { 
                            s 
                            }

                            { 
                            s

                            u 
                            }

                            =

                               a

                               b

                               c 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      .

                               K

                       of the knowledge state

                       those elements

                            B

                               OM 
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                       of the knowledge state  
                         K 
                      , to make the subsequent skill-pushing scheme more rigorous. Furthermore, we know that for a student with knowledge state  
                         K

                               ′

                                  T

                            = 
                            p 
                            
                               ( 
                               T

                            B

                               T 
                            
                            
                               ′

So far, we can use the skill function to characterize the bottom/top space of the competence state and the problem function to characterize the (competence-based) outer/inner master fringe of the knowledge state in the competence model. Of course, in the competence-based knowledge space theory, it is not enough to only obtain the outer/inner master fringe of the knowledge states; it is also necessary to push or recommend the corresponding skill sets for learning and consolidation, as shown in  Theorems 11 and 12  below.

Remark 8 
                         (1) From an educational perspective, the competence-based inner master fringe of  
                               K 
                             represents the high point of a student’s knowledge state. This is the set of problems that the student has recently mastered, which may not be fully solidified and may require review and reinforcement. Therefore, if a student in knowledge state  
                               K 
                             experiences a regression, their knowledge state is most likely to regress to  
                               
                                  K 
                                  ∖ 
                                  B 
                               
                            , where  
                               
                                  B 
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        IM 
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            , and their regressed competence state  
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     B 
                                  
                               
                             satisfies  
                               
                                  p 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           B 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  K 
                                  ∖ 
                                  B 
                               
                            . According to  Theorem 12 , this student can consolidate or surpass their prior knowledge state  
                               K 
                             by learning only the skill set  
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     0 
                                  
                               
                            , where  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  T 
                               
                            . Here, “consolidate” and “surpass” mean that after learning  
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     0 
                                  
                               
                            , the student’s competence state  
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     B 
                                  
                               
                             becomes  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        B 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∪ 
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            , and their knowledge state  
                               
                                  p 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           B 
                                        
                                     
                                     ∪ 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             will equal  
                               K 
                             (consolidate) or properly contain  
                               K 
                             (surpass). 
                         (2) All problem sets  
                               B 
                             in the competence-based inner master fringe  
                               
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                                  
                                     IM 
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                             are prone to be forgotten and require reinforcement. 
                         (3) To determine whether a student has reinforced the knowledge state  
                               K 
                            , the problem set  
                               
                                  ∪

                                        IM 
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             can be used for assessment.

In competence-based knowledge space theory, we obtain the problem sets, and the skill sets through consulting experts or other means, as well as the corresponding relationship between problems and skills, that is, the skill function (skill map, skill multimap). This induces the corresponding problem function and knowledge structure, then utilizes the knowledge structure to test and obtain the students’ knowledge states, and finally, based on the knowledge state, obtains the master fringe and provides learning guidance and reinforcement learning recommendations. This last step is the focus of this paper’s research.

Next, we will introduce the relevant content of “leveraging outer master fringe to provide learning guidance” through a practical example, covering three aspects: guidance strategies, guidance schemes, and advantages of guidance strategies and schemes over existing ones. In this paper, guidance schemes refer to the skill sets that students need to master next, while guidance strategies refer to the methods for generating these guidance schemes. Specifically,  Theorem 11  presents a guidance strategy, and  Example 9  demonstrates the use of this guidance strategy. The part (3) of  Example 9  is the generated guidance scheme. Similarly, the content related to “leveraging inner master fringe to provide reinforcement learning recommendations” is analogous and will not be elaborated further. For comparative purposes, we have selected a specific example from  Stefanutti and de Chiusole (2017) . As shown in  Table 1 , this example is a conjunctive model. Since the subsequent discussion involves the outer fringe, we exclude problems  
                         
                            
                               q 
                            
                            
                               7 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               q 
                            
                            
                               12 
                            
                         
                       from the example below to ensure the generated knowledge structure is discriminative. Skipping several preceding steps, assume that through testing, the teacher obtains the students’ knowledge state as  
                         K 
                      . According to existing research, strategies for providing learning guidance can be categorized into the following four cases:

(1) When there is no introduction of skills and the outer fringe of the knowledge state is non-empty, guidance is generally provided using the outer fringe of the knowledge state (denoted as strategy (a);  Falmagne and Doignon (2011) ). It is worth noting that the outer fringe is a special case of the outer master fringe. Therefore, in this case, the guidance schemes using the outer master fringe and the outer fringe of the knowledge state are consistent.

(2) When there is no introduction of skills and the outer fringe of the knowledge state is empty, if the guidance is restricted to recommending only one problem for learning at a time without altering the problem domain, it is not possible to provide learning guidance using the outer fringe of the knowledge state (i.e., strategy (a)). In this scenario, considering the master fringe makes sense and the solution proposed in this paper is to use the outer master fringe of the knowledge state for providing learning guidance (denoted as strategy (b)).

(3) When skills are introduced and the effective outer fringe of the competence state is non-empty, guidance is generally provided using the effective outer fringe of the competence state (denoted as strategy (c);  Stefanutti and de Chiusole (2017) ). For example, in this case, when the knowledge state is

                         g

                         h 
                       (“find a common divisor”), but such a problem does not exist because every problem involving fraction reduction inherently entails identifying a common divisor between the numerator and the denominator ( Stefanutti & de Chiusole, 2017 ). Therefore, students can only learn skill  
                         h 
                       first and then learn skill  
                         g 
                      , indicating a partial order relation between these two skills. After using the newly modified test as a guiding basis, this situation can be categorized under the above-mentioned strategy (c).
