It is worth noting that, it is not that the strategy for generating guidance schemes in solution two (i.e., strategy (c)) is superior to that of solution one (i.e., strategy (d)), but rather that solution two used the new test with higher accuracy (referred to as improving an existing test ( Anselmi et al., 2022 ); the accuracy of the test is not rigorously defined in this paper; it can be loosely understood as fewer elements in the equivalence class of the competence state leading to higher accuracy). According to (3), if solution one were to use the same new test as a guiding basis, their guidance schemes would align. Furthermore, according to the findings of  Anselmi et al. (2022) , tests can be further improved, enabling accurate assessment of all competence states. Lastly, strategy (c) is specific to conjunctive models, whereas strategy (d) can be applied to more general competence models.

In summary, for various types of tests, if learning guidance can be provided based on existing research, then using the competence-based outer master fringe of the knowledge state can also provide learning guidance, and the provided guidance schemes will encompass existing ones. Moreover, in cases where learning guidance cannot be provided based on existing research, utilizing the competence-based outer master fringe of the knowledge state can still offer learning guidance.

This paper provides a new characterization of the master fringe based on the perspective of competence. For a given knowledge state, corresponding skills can be pushed for different models to reach its adjacent state or consolidate the current state. Building upon this, a general method for personalized learning guidance (personalized reinforcement learning recommendation) is proposed.

The process of learning guidance via the competence model involves: first, obtaining the current knowledge state through testing; then, using  Theorems 4 and 8  to obtain the competence-based outer master fringe of the current knowledge state, as demonstrated in  Example 6 ; next, allowing students to choose a problem set from it to form the target knowledge state with the current knowledge state, and then obtaining the skill set family  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               B 
                            
                         
                       through  Theorem 11 , as demonstrated in  Example 9 ; finally, letting students choose a skill set from it for learning, which can achieve or exceed the target knowledge state and can be verified using the selected problem set.

The process of reinforcement learning recommendation via the competence model involves: first, obtaining the current knowledge state through testing; then, using  Theorems 5 and 10  to obtain the competence-based inner master fringe of the current knowledge state, as demonstrated in  Example 8 , where all problem sets in the inner master fringe need to be consolidated; next, obtaining the skill set family  
                         T 
                       through  Theorem 12 , as demonstrated in  Example 10 ; finally, letting students choose a skill set from it for consolidation learning, which can maintain or exceed the current knowledge state and can be verified using problem sets in the inner master fringe.

With the implementation of personalized learning guidance, it logically follows that we can offer a method for personalized learning path recommendations: Following the learning guidance, the process is repeated with the target knowledge state as the current knowledge state until the student’s knowledge state encompasses the full set. This repetition constitutes a personalized learning path. During each session of learning guidance, there are two opportunities for choice: one is selecting a problem set from the outer master fringe, and the other is selecting a skill set from  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               B 
                            
                         
                      . Considering all possible choices, we can derive all learning paths. Then, by combining them with the student’s needs (such as minimizing the total learning time or minimizing the average number of skills learned per step), we can select the optimal path.
